key_strengths: $176B insurance float funded at zero or negative cost — the largest in the world and near-irreplicable
60-year compounding record at 19.7% annually vs. 10.5% S&P 500 — longest sustained outperformance in public markets
$373B cash fortress covering total debt nearly 3x — the most secure large-cap balance sheet in existence
Greg Abel compensation: entire $15M after-tax salary committed to BRK share purchases annually, zero SBC
BNSF and BHE are individually moated, essential-infrastructure businesses with durable regulated earnings
FY2025 operating earnings of $44.49B and $25.1B FCF across a diversified empire of moat businesses
Beta of ~0.65 — exceptional capital preservation profile for a $1T+ conglomerate
Preferred acquirer status for private businesses provides proprietary deal pipeline unavailable to financial buyers
key_risks: Greg Abel unproven as capital allocator at Berkshire's scale — only 10 weeks into the role as of this analysis
$373B cash hoard earns T-bill returns; if rates fall to 2%, investment income drops ~$8-9B annually
BHE PacifiCorp wildfire liability ($8B+ in claims, potential trebling) — ring-fenced but book value impairment risk is real
Insurance underwriting profit declined 19% to $7.26B in 2025; management is deliberately reducing P&C volume further
Multiple simultaneous transitions: new CEO (Abel), CFO retiring 2027, Todd Combs departed to JPMorgan
red_flags: No share repurchases during all of 2025 — management's revealed preference showed no cheap entry until $492 in March 2026
BNSF operating ratio materially worse than Union Pacific — Abel called the gap 'unacceptable' but fixes take years
GEICO underwriting profit under pressure from rising acquisition costs as Progressive gains market share
Apple position (~$85-90B, ~32% of equity portfolio) still dominant despite substantial trimming
buy_triggers: Stock falls to 1.2-1.3x book (~$399-432), restoring the margin of safety Berkshire's own repurchase history defines as cheap
Abel completes a large, clearly value-accretive acquisition demonstrating capital allocation judgment
Insurance underwriting normalizes after a quiet catastrophe year, restoring operating earnings to $50B+
sell_triggers: Abel introduces stock-based compensation or discretionary bonuses, signaling cultural drift from the owner-operator model
Abel executes a large acquisition at clearly excessive multiples, prioritizing empire-building over per-share value
Insurance float shows a multi-year pattern of underwriting losses structurally reversing the negative-cost float model
Stock reaches 2.0x book (~$665) without commensurate intrinsic value growth — trim at that level
valuation_summary: At $492/BRK-B and 1.48x book, sum-of-parts analysis ($369B cash + $264B equity portfolio + operating businesses at 13-15x ex-investment income) supports a fair-value range of $456-$501/share, placing the current price near the midpoint of intrinsic value with thin price margin of safety but exceptional business margin of safety from the fortress balance sheet; the buyback resumption in March 2026 provides a soft management-endorsed floor near $492.
source_summary: Web search results (March 2026) for current price, P/B ratio, and FY2025/Q4 2025 earnings data; Berkshire Hathaway FY2025 annual report press release (Feb 28, 2026) and Q3 2025 10-Q for balance sheet and segment data; Greg Abel's 2025 shareholder letter and CNBC coverage of Abel's personal stock purchase and buyback restart (March 5-7, 2026); GuruFocus, MacroTrends, StockAnalysis, and Morningstar for valuation multiples; training knowledge for structural analysis of the float model and historical capital allocation record.
